response_text:
# JavaScript's Concurrency Model

## The Direct Answer

**JavaScript has no built-in multi-threading.** It is fundamentally **single-threaded** — there is one call stack, one memory heap, and one thread of execution per JavaScript environment (browser tab, Node.js process, etc.).

---

## The Actual Model: Event Loop + Concurrency Illusion

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  JS Engine (Single Thread)           │
│                                                      │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  Call Stack  │    │       Memory Heap        │    │
│  │              │    │  (objects, closures...)  │    │
│  │  main()      │    │                          │    │
│  │  setTimeout  │    └─────────────────────────┘    │
│  │  ...         │                                    │
│  └──────┬───────┘                                    │
│         │                                            │
└─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Event Loop                        │
│                                                      │
│   Microtask Queue        Macrotask Queue             │
│  ┌──────────────┐       ┌──────────────┐            │
│  │ Promise.then │       │ setTimeout   │            │
│  │ queueMicro.. │       │ setInterval  │            │
│  │ MutationObs  │       │ I/O events   │            │
│  └──────────────┘       └──────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### How the Event Loop Works

```javascript
console.log("1 - sync");                          // Runs immediately

setTimeout(() => console.log("2 - macrotask"), 0); // Que
